title: practical english usage - Part 298
content:
I finished early because I worked fast.

and       its     clause           can       go     after        or    before           the      main         clause.

2 position      of  because-clauses I'm happy because of you.

- I'm happy because I met you.

We were late because of the rain, (     ... because the-rain.) not - We were late because it rained, (     ... because of it rained;) pronoun.              Compare: and       verb.                      is    a   two-word              preposition,                  used         before           a   noun         or    a Because of is    a   conjunction.                   It  is    used         at    the      beginning                 of    a   clause,          before           a   subject Because 1 because         and   because         of 393 because beat - beat - beaten win - won - won Both       verbs         are      irregular: me at poker...) not My girlfriend beat me at poker the first time we played, (     My   girlfriend won My girlfriend usually wins when we play poker.

etc      against.           Compare: prize,        money,           etc.     You       can              a   person            that       you       are      playing/arguing/fighting, beat You       can            (in)   a   game,         a   race,       a   battle,         an     argument,               etc,     and       you       can           a win                             win 392              and beat         win ►   -nr,- bear vocabulary                  •  394                           etc:     changes become, get, go, grow, For more about get, ►    472-473,  108.

change            is    gradual.                 is    more         informal              than               and Go            turn  grow.

can       also       be     used         in    these         cases          (see      below),         and       so     can                when          the Turn                            grow Other         examples: go blue with cold / red with embarrassment / green with envy.

Suddenly everything went black and I lost consciousness.

She went white with anger.

Leaves go brown in autumn,  (     Leaver get brown...) in    British          English.

(and      not             is    used         to    talk       about          changes              of    colour,          especially Go     get) a  colours style.       This     is    common               in    two       cases.

can       be     used         before           adjectives                 to    talk       about          change,            especially                 in    an     informal Go 4 go    red,    go    mad,     etc She's nice when you get to know her.

After a few weeks I got to like the job better.

We    can       sometimes                  use             with        an     infinitive               to    talk       about          a   gradual             change.

3 get    + infinitive For get used to, ►    615.

I became a grandfather last week, (     I g&t-a grandfather...) is    not      normally               used         before           nouns           to    talk       about          changes.

for     the      worse,          like                   (BrE)   /              (►   394.4 below).

We    generally               use            not            to    talk       about          changes              of    colour           and       some          changes go,  get, They got married in 1986, and got divorced two years later.

can       also       be     used         before           past        participles                 like Get                      lost, broken, dressed, married.

You get younger every day.

It was getting very dark, (informal)             is    very        common               before           adjectives                 (without            nouns).

2  get    dark,     younger,         etc not Please get ready now. (     Please become ready now.) is    not      usually            used         to    talk       about          single          deliberate                actions.
